title: Chapter 18: Leo in the Storm
content: I ran.

Not from the fire. Not from the twisted metal of the car or the blackened grass or the stunned silence of the road behind me. From the release. That's what had me shaking — the sheer, vomiting force of letting go of something I'd been clenching for weeks, maybe months, maybe my whole life. The fire had come out of me like a scream made physical, and now that it was gone, there was nothing left to hold me upright. I was hollow. I was a pipe that had finally let the water through, and the water had taken everything with it.

I didn't decide to run into the forest. My legs decided for me, carrying me across the road, over the ditch, and into the trees before my mind could mount an objection. Not that it tried. My mind had gone quiet sometime around the third scream, and it hadn't come back online yet. There was only my body, moving, and the trees, receiving me.

The fog stopped at the tree line. I noticed that first — the way it pooled and curdled at the edge of the forest like water refusing to soak into oilcloth. The trees were too old, too rooted, too real for it. Their trunks rose out of the earth with an authority the fog couldn't argue with, and so it didn't try. It waited at the boundary like something that knew its place. I kept going.

My feet knew the path before my mind did. Roots and stones and the soft give of rain-darkened needles, and I was deep enough within minutes that the road felt like something I'd dreamed. My hands were still smoking. Faint threads of blue, like breath on a cold morning, curling off my fingers and vanishing before they reached my wrists. I tucked them under my arms and kept walking, the fabric of my shirt damp against the strange coolness of whatever was leaking out of my skin.

The oak found me before I found it. The same oak — I knew without checking, the same gnarled ancestor from that first night when I'd fled the cottage with a journal I couldn't read and a name I couldn't stop hearing. The bark was cool when I pressed my back against it. Cool and rough and alive in a way that asked nothing of me. The tree didn't want me to solve anything. It didn't need me to be strong or brave or the latest iteration of a two-hundred-and-sixty-year catastrophe. It just stood there, being a tree, and I let myself slide down its trunk until I was sitting in the dirt with my knees pulled to my chest.

Pine. Rain. The faint green musk of moss and slow decomposition, that rich forest-floor smell of things being broken down and remade. The forest smelled like a place where things were allowed to die and rot and become something else, and there was no shame in any of it. No performance. No legacy. Just the long, quiet business of being alive and then not and then alive again in a different form. And the silence — not true silence, I could hear the drip of water from the canopy and the creak of old wood settling and the distant percussion of a woodpecker somewhere to the east — but the silence of a place that didn't demand I speak. That didn't need me to explain. That would go on being exactly what it was whether I answered its questions or not.

My hands kept smoking. I watched them the way you watch a wound you can't believe you sustained, turning them over in the half-light, watching the blue wisps rise and dissipate. They didn't burn. They didn't even feel warm. They just leaked something I didn't have a name for, and I was too tired to be afraid of it. Too tired to be anything. I sat against that oak and felt the trembling work through me like a second heartbeat, and I let it. There was no one to see. No one to perform for. Just the tree and the smoke and the silence that didn't care.

I didn't hear him coming. That was the thing about Leo — he didn't arrive, he just was. One moment I was alone with the oak and the smoke, and the next there was a man standing six feet away with an armload of firewood, and amber light glowing through the trees behind him where his cabin sat like a held breath in the dark. The window cast a warm rectangle across the underbrush, and I could see the silhouette of something on the sill — a book, a cup, the small artifacts of a life being lived without apology.

He saw my hands. I watched his face do something complicated — not fear, not the flinch I'd seen on strangers my whole life when they encountered something they couldn't categorize. Recognition. Like a man seeing a word in a language he'd forgotten he knew. His eyes tracked the blue smoke and something behind them clicked into place, the way a lock accepts a key, and then the expression was gone, smoothed away under the quiet calm he wore like a second skin. He didn't comment. He didn't step back. He looked at my hands the way he looked at the forest — like they belonged here, like they were part of the landscape.

He put down the firewood. One piece at a time, stacking it against a fallen log with the careful deliberateness of a man who understood that how you do a small thing matters. Then he walked over. Not to me — nearby. A few feet to my left, where another root made a natural seat against the oak's base. He sat down. Leaned back. Looked up at the canopy like he had nowhere else to be and no one else to be it with.

The same pattern as before. As that first night, as the night I'd fled the cottage and found the forest and found him. But the pattern was different this time, because the direction was different. I hadn't run from something. I'd run to it. To the forest. To the stillness. To the amber light and the man who lived inside it.

We sat like that for long enough that the smoking stopped. Long enough that the woodpecker moved on and the dripping slowed and the light behind the cabin's window shifted — the fire inside catching, growing. He didn't ask what happened. He didn't ask if I was okay. He didn't ask anything, and the not-asking was the most generous thing anyone had done for me in months.

What broke me wasn't a question. It was the steady, even rhythm of his breathing. In and out, in and out, like the tide, like something you could set a clock to, and I realized I couldn't remember the last time I'd breathed like that. Like it was easy. Like my body knew how to do it without being told. Everyone in my life wanted something from my lungs — air for spells, words for answers, silence for compliance. Leo just breathed beside me like breathing was enough.

The sob came from somewhere below my chest, below my ribs, somewhere I didn't know existed until it cracked open. It wasn't graceful. It wasn't the controlled, literary crying I'd read about in the Aurelia journals — the single tear rolling down a pale cheek, the trembling lip, the beautiful devastation of a woman undone. The Aurelias had made even their grief look like art, and I'd tried, God, I'd tried to grieve the way they taught me. But this was ugly. This was snot and gasping and a sound that came out of me like something with teeth, and I couldn't stop it, and I couldn't shape it, and I couldn't make it pretty, and the journals had never once told me what to do with a feeling that wouldn't behave.

He didn't flinch. He didn't reach for me. He waited until the worst of it had passed — until I was shaking with the aftermath of it, hollow and wrung out and too empty even for embarrassment — and then he opened his arms. Not grandly. Not like a gesture designed to be witnessed. Just opened them, the way you open a door. Whether I walked through was up to me.
I walked through.

His arms were warm. That's what I remember most — the simple, animal warmth of another person's body, steady and certain. He smelled like pine and rain and bread. Bread. He'd been baking. The faint sweetness of yeast and flour, the kind of smell that meant someone was in the middle of making something, was engaged in the small, necessary work of turning raw things into nourishment. Not curse-breaking. Not boundary-walking. Bread. I pressed my face into his shoulder and the smell of it — pine and rain and the warm yeasted comfort of a kitchen in use — undid me all over again, and I cried until I couldn't cry anymore.

He held me. He didn't try to fix it. He didn't murmur soothing words or tell me it would be all right or ask me to explain what was wrong so he could solve it. He just held me, one hand moving in slow circles on my back, the other steady on my shoulder, and he let me fall apart without trying to gather the pieces. As if breaking was allowed. As if I didn't have to be whole to be held. As if a woman in pieces was still a woman, still welcome, still worth the warmth of another person's arms.

The crying stopped eventually. Not because I was done — I wasn't done, I was years from done — but because my body ran out of the resources to keep going. I stayed where I was anyway, my cheek against his collarbone, his heartbeat slow and unbothered beneath my ear. The forest settled around us. The rain softened to a mist that beaded on his jacket and caught the last of the amber light from the cabin window.
"Come inside," he said. His voice was low, rumbling through his chest and into mine. Not a command. An offering. A door, held open.
I nodded against him, and he helped me up, and we walked through the trees toward the amber light.

The cabin was small. I registered that first — the close, deliberate smallness of a place built for one, built to be enough. A single room with a sleeping loft and a stove and a table that seated two only if they didn't mind bumping elbows. It was warm. The stove in the corner was lit, iron panels radiating heat into the room, and the crackle of the fire inside it was the only sound for a long moment — the popping and hissing of wood giving itself over to something larger than itself. The sound filled the room the way a heartbeat fills a body: constantly, unremarkably, essential.

Books were everywhere. Stacked on the table, wedged between jars on the shelf, open and face-down on the windowsill as if he'd been reading three at once and refused to choose between them. Botany. Geology. Folklore. A battered field guide to lichens. And one, cracked and spine-broken, that I recognized: *Antiquities of the North Riding*, the same obscure local history I'd found in the mobile library, its pages bristling with scraps of paper and dried leaves used as bookmarks.
But it was the wall that stopped me.

A map. Hand-drawn, painstakingly detailed, pinned to the plaster with brass tacks that had left small dark wounds in the limewash. The Boundary Stones. All twelve of them, plotted in their positions around Blackwood Vale, with lines connecting them in patterns I'd never considered — not a circle, not a grid, but something older, something that looked almost like a constellation. Notes in Leo's cramped handwriting clustered around each point. Dates. Measurements. Symbols I didn't recognize but that had been researched enough to fill the margins with cross-references and question marks and one urgent notation I couldn't quite read from where I stood.

"I told you nobody knows what they're for," he said from behind me. I could hear him hanging his coat on the hook by the door. The small, domestic sound of it — brass on wood, fabric settling. "I lied. I've been trying to figure it out."

He didn't explain why. He didn't tell me the story I could see written in the map's careful lines — the years of work it represented, the nights spent measuring and mapping and walking the boundary alone. He didn't tell me what it meant that he'd been doing this, that the night I met him he'd been walking the stones like I'd been walking them, that his calm was not the calm of a man who didn't understand the world he lived in but the calm of a man who understood it too well and had decided to do something about it quietly, on his own, without asking anyone's permission.
He didn't have to.
"Sit," he said, pulling out a chair at the small table. "I was making pie."

He was. There was dough rolled out on the floured counter, apples sliced and sugared in a bowl, the whole apparatus of it suspended mid-process. He went back to it like it was the most natural thing in the world — a woman with smoke-blue hands sitting in his kitchen, a map of cursed stones on his wall, and apple pie to be finished. He didn't choose between the impossible and the ordinary. He just did both.

I sat. I watched him. The competence of his hands, the economy of his movements, the way he crimped the edges of the crust with a fork without looking down. The stove crackled and popped. Rain tapped at the window like a visitor too polite to knock properly. The amber light from the lamp made everything look like it had been painted in honey, and for a moment I understood what Julian saw when he looked at the world — the beauty of it, the way light could turn a small cluttered kitchen into a cathedral. But Julian would have painted it and called it his. Leo just lived in it and called it enough.

He slid the pie into the oven and set a timer — an old mechanical one, the kind that ticks like a small, dedicated heart — and then he turned a chair around and sat across from me, arms folded on the backrest, and looked at me with those steady, unhurried eyes. Not studying me the way Julian studied a subject. Just seeing me. Just being present for whatever I was about to be.
One question. That's all he asked.
"What do you need?"

Not *what happened*. Not *are you okay*. Not *how did you do that* or *what does it mean* or any of the hundred questions I could see banked behind his calm like coals behind a grate. What do you need. Right now. In this room. From this person. A question that assumed I knew the answer. A question that trusted me to know myself.
I opened my mouth. Closed it. Opened it again. The words came from the same place the sob had come from — deep, unguarded, past all the careful architecture I'd built to keep myself presentable.
"I need," I said, and my voice was a ruin, "to not be the seventh woman in a two-hundred-and-sixty-year curse for about ten minutes."

He nodded. Slowly, like he was filing that information exactly where it belonged — not in a drawer labeled *problems to solve*, not in a folder marked *things to fix*, but somewhere warmer, somewhere that could hold it without trying to change its shape. And then he said:
"You can be the girl eating pie in my kitchen. That's not cursed."

The timer went off forty minutes later. Forty minutes of the tick and the crackle and the smell of apples and butter and sugar transfiguring themselves inside the oven, the cabin filling with a warmth that had nothing to do with temperature. Forty minutes of sitting across from a man who didn't need me to be anyone in particular, who seemed perfectly content to share a room with a stranger who was trying to become less strange to herself. The pie emerged golden and steaming, and he set it on the table with two forks and no plates, and I ate.

It was good. God, it was good — the butter-flake of the crust dissolving on my tongue, the soft-collapse of the apples, the cinnamon and sugar and something else, something green and bright I couldn't name that might have been the wild apples themselves or might have been the hands that made the pie. I ate like I hadn't eaten in days, which maybe I hadn't, forkful after forkful, not caring that I was making small sounds of satisfaction, not caring that my eyes were leaking again. The taste of it — apple and spice and the salt of my own tears falling into the crust without my permission — was so ordinary and so essential that I started crying again, quieter this time, the kind of crying that doesn't interrupt anything, just runs alongside it like a stream beside a path.

He pretended not to notice. He ate his own piece slowly, methodically, and got up to make tea, and set a cup in front of me without comment, and the whole time he let me cry into that pie like it was the most normal thing in the world. A girl, eating pie, crying. Nothing to fix. Nothing to explain. Nothing to perform. Nothing to be but exactly what I was.
He was right. It wasn't cursed.
Later — much later, back at the cottage with the journals spread around me and the fog pressing at the windows like a visitor who hadn't been invited — I wrote:
*The ranger held me while I fell apart and didn't try to reassemble me. The artist painted me as he wanted me to be. One of them asked what I needed. One of them told me what I needed.*
*I know which one I'm going back to.*
*But not yet. The curse isn't done with me, and I'm not done with it.*
